title: Who Is Half-Baked, Jaya or Me?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. The Tamil section is a source-checked reading copy derived from the OCR and does not alter the canonical source file. Visual review restored the opening `உடன்பிறப்பே,`, omitted by OCR. Other corrections are limited to unambiguous word-join and glyph errors, including `பத்திரிகா தர்மம்` and `நடுநிலையாளர்கள்`; the political claims and quoted register descriptions remain attributed as in the source. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: In my statement of 27-6-2014, I referred to proceedings in the Kerala Legislative Assembly. Chief Minister Oommen Chandy had declared there: “The four dams, Mullaperiyar, Parambikulam, Thunakkadavu and Peruvaripallam, are now under Kerala’s control. They belong entirely to Kerala. Until 2009, the national register of large dams listed all four as belonging to Tamil Nadu. Following Kerala’s strong objection, the entry was changed in 2012 to show that they belong to Kerala.”

I therefore asked: Tamil Nadu Chief Minister Jayalalithaa repeatedly claims, “I alone uphold Tamil Nadu’s river-water rights.” When Oommen Chandy asserts that these four dams belong to Kerala, through whose efforts was that claim established?

How should Jayalalithaa have answered? She should have said that Kerala Chief Minister Oommen Chandy’s declaration was entirely wrong and condemned it. Or she should have explained what action the Tamil Nadu government had taken, or was taking, in response.

Instead of giving a constructive answer and explaining this vital issue to the people of Tamil Nadu, Jayalalithaa poured irritation and anger upon me for bringing it to public attention. In keeping with her own political culture, she accused me of “turning a louse into a nit and the nit into a deity,” invoked the saying “an angry person has little sense,” and called mine a “half-baked statement.”

Some Tamil Nadu newspapers, afraid to publish my statement, prominently carried only Jayalalithaa’s reply in order to please her. Everyone in the world can watch and admire the way journalistic ethics flies its victorious flag in Tamil Nadu!

Jayalalithaa says the 2009 National Register of Large Dams did not include latitude and longitude details, that these were newly added in 2012, and that a footnote nevertheless made it clear that the four dams were operated and maintained by Tamil Nadu.

Was Jayalalithaa not Tamil Nadu’s Chief Minister in 2012? When the four dams were added to Kerala’s list in the national register that year, should she not have raised her voice in protest? Should she not have conveyed her objection to the Union government? Those in office naturally have access to every detail of the national register. What justice is there in asking me, then in opposition, why I did not object in 2012? Let impartial observers consider it.

Jayalalithaa says that, at the insistence of the AIADMK government, the 2013 national register clearly added a footnote stating: “These four dams are operated and maintained by the Tamil Nadu Public Works Department.” But in the same statement she says that both the 2009 and 2012 registers, like the 2013 footnote, already stated that the four dams were operated and maintained by Tamil Nadu. If that is true, her own statement exposes the emptiness of claiming credit and pride that the 2013 clarification resulted only from the AIADMK government’s insistence.

Jayalalithaa says that describing the Periyar dam as Kerala’s would amount to insulting the Supreme Court, which held that it belongs to Tamil Nadu. That accusation applies to Kerala Chief Minister Oommen Chandy’s declaration, not to me for bringing it to public attention.

Jayalalithaa, whose thoughts remain forever fixed on the Bengaluru Special Court hearing the disproportionate-assets case, looks rather amusing when she tries to frighten me with the spectre of contempt of court.

Instead of pouncing upon and clawing at me for informing Tamil Nadu’s people of Oommen Chandy’s Assembly statement, Jayalalithaa should correct her uncivilised approach. At least now, through proper efforts, she must ensure that the national register identifies all four dams as belonging to Tamil Nadu.

There is a proverb about asking the way to Pattukkottai and being told that ten betel nuts cost so much. Jayalalithaa’s attempt to conduct the state administration in that fashion can bring the people of Tamil Nadu no benefit.

My statement also referred to the Kerala Assembly debate on linking Kerala’s Pamba and Achankovil rivers with Tamil Nadu’s Vaippar. In her reply, Jayalalithaa cited the Supreme Court’s February 2012 judgment on implementing river-linking. Though she concealed it, those familiar with the issue will remember that the judgment resulted from a case filed in the Madras High Court in 1983 by DMK advocate K.S. Radhakrishnan and subsequently pursued on appeal in the Supreme Court.

Unable to answer my statement directly but needing to say something circuitous, Jayalalithaa vaguely claimed: “I personally pressed the Prime Minister on river-linking.” She magnified her role as though she alone had taken deep interest and initiated action for the first time. What is the truth?

The DMK government’s 2007-2008 Budget itself stated: “To prevent river-water sharing disputes from arising in ways that injure the unity, integrity and sovereignty of India’s states, this government will continue vigorous efforts to have the Union government urgently begin work on linking at least the peninsular rivers.” I also spoke at length at a National Development Council meeting to draw the attention of the Union government and every state government to river-linking.

We did not stop with a Budget announcement. To avert flood danger, prevent floodwater from running wastefully into the sea, and carry water to drought regions, the first project in India to link rivers flowing within a state was undertaken in Tamil Nadu under DMK rule.

Under the Cauvery-Gundar link project, intended to carry surplus water to dry districts, work was approved and undertaken on a Rs. 189 crore barrage across the Cauvery at Kattalai near Mayanur. The Tamiraparani-Karumeniyar-Nambiyar link project was approved at an estimated Rs. 369 crore and work proceeded. A Rs. 174 crore proposal to link the Cheyyar with the Pennaiyar was sent for consideration by the Union government’s Central Water Commission.

Jayalalithaa now speaks herself hoarse about river-linking. Will she explain what efforts the AIADMK government has made to continue and complete, from where the DMK government left them, the Cauvery-Gundar and Tamiraparani-Karumeniyar-Nambiyar projects?
I trust that the people of Tamil Nadu will now understand who issues half-baked statements in anger, Jayalalithaa or me.
With affection,
M.K.
